forecasts.reasoning: The base rate for a Dow Jones Industrial Average component change is roughly one event every 2 to 3 years. The forecast horizon is quite short, spanning just over two months from April 25, 2025, to July 1, 2025. This yields a baseline probability of around 6-8%. In 2024, the DJIA underwent two separate reshuffles (adding Amazon, Nvidia, and Sherwin-Williams), which further depresses the likelihood that the index committee would orchestrate another change so soon without a forcing function. None of the listed 30 mega-cap companies are apparent candidates for sudden bankruptcy or outright acquisitions that would force a removal in the next two months. Without access to search tools to verify any very recent announcements from April 2025, adhering to the base-rate of 7% is the most rigorously calibrated estimate.

The timeframe from the knowledge cutoff (April 25, 2025) to the resolution date (July 1, 2025) is barely over two months. Revoking a 501(c)(3) status requires following strict IRS administrative procedures, which include audits, notices of proposed revocation, appeals, and eventual final determinations. This process typically takes years, not months. Even if the Trump administration attempts to bypass the standard IRS rules to revoke Harvard's status immediately, Harvard has the legal resources to file for an emergency injunction in federal court the very next day. Courts would almost certainly issue a stay or preliminary injunction, as a rushed revocation would likely violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and due process protections, keeping Harvard's tax-exempt status legally intact while the issue is litigated. Any attempt to use legislation would take significant time, likely require 60 Senate votes to bypass a filibuster (Republicans only hold 53 seats), and face severe constitutional challenges such as being an unconstitutional bill of attainder. Due to the extremely tight timeline and the existence of administrative and judicial safeguards, it is highly implausible that Harvard will definitively lose its 501(c)(3) status before July 1, 2025. I am placing my probability at the minimum allowed bound of 0.05.

Based on recent historical base rates, the frequency of successful coups in Africa was relatively high from 2020 to 2023 (with approximately 8 successful coups, or ~2 per year). However, 2024 saw a lull with 0 successful coups, suggesting the contagion wave in the Sahel may have peaked. Latin America's rate of successful coups remains negligible, primarily experiencing failed attempts. Assuming the overall base rate has receded to roughly 1.0–1.2 successful coups per year across these regions, we must estimate for the time window starting from the question's likely generation date (late January 2025, given the 'Chad January 2025 attempt' reference) until July 1, 2025. This 5-month window (~0.41 years) implies an expected frequency of 0.4 to 0.5 events. Using a Poisson distribution (1 - exp(-lambda)), the baseline probability of at least one successful coup occurring in a 5-month span is roughly 33-39%. Factoring in the recent cooling of the coup trend in 2024, I set the final probability at 34%.
